title: Crème Caramel
subtitle: Baked vanilla egg custard turned out over a pool of liquid caramel
excerpt: A smooth egg custard baked in a caramel-lined mould, then chilled and turned out so the caramel flows over the top as a sauce. The caramel is made first and sets at the base of the ramekin; the custard goes on top; the whole is baked in a bain-marie until just trembling.
sourceNotes: Crème caramel appears across French, Spanish (flan), and Latin American culinary traditions. The technique of lining a mould with caramel, filling with custard, baking, and inverting is consistent across all versions. The French version, set in individual ramekins rather than a large mould, is the form most common in British home cooking.

recipe.batchNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Keep the ramekins covered in the fridge and turn out just before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made at least 6 hours ahead and can be refrigerated for up to three days before turning out.

content.text: The caramel is the part that requires attention. It goes from amber to black very quickly in the final few seconds, and black caramel is bitter rather than sweet. Take the pan off the heat when the caramel reaches a deep amber, and work quickly to coat each ramekin before it sets. The caramel will continue to darken from residual heat even after the pan is off the hob.

The custard must be strained before going into the ramekins to remove any threads of cooked egg white or vanilla seed clusters. A custard that hasn't been strained will have a slightly uneven texture. The bain-marie protects the custard from the direct heat of the oven, without it, the eggs would scramble.
Six 150 ml ramekins, warmed slightly so the caramel stays liquid long enough to coat.

Put 150 g of caster sugar in a clean, heavy-based saucepan with 3 tablespoons of water. Heat over medium heat without stirring until the sugar dissolves, then raise the heat and cook without stirring until the caramel reaches a deep amber colour. Remove from the heat immediately and carefully pour a thin layer into each ramekin, tilting to coat the base. The caramel will set within a minute. Work quickly.

Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Warm {{whole-milk}} of whole milk and {{double-cream}} of double cream together in a saucepan until steaming but not boiling. Whisk 3 whole eggs, 2 egg yolks, 75 g of caster sugar, and {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract together in a large bowl until combined. Pour the warm milk and cream in slowly, whisking as you go. Strain through a fine sieve into a jug.

Divide the custard evenly between the caramel-lined ramekins. Set the ramekins in a deep roasting tin and pour just-boiled water into the tin to come halfway up the sides. Bake for 35, 40 minutes until the custard is just set with a faint tremble at the centre. Remove from the water bath, cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 6 hours.

Run a thin knife around the inside edge of each ramekin. Place a deep serving plate over the ramekin and invert firmly. Hold for a few seconds, you will hear the custard release. Lift the ramekin away. The caramel will flow over and around the custard as a sauce. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Caramel is bitter and very dark
items.fix: Watch the colour carefully in the final 30 seconds, the change from amber to dark happens fast. Take the pan off the heat at amber, not dark brown.

items.symptom: Custard has air bubbles on the surface
items.cause: Mixture was poured too fast or whisked too vigorously
items.fix: Pour the custard slowly. If there are bubbles on the surface before baking, pop them with a blowtorch or skim with a spoon.

items.symptom: Custard did not release from the ramekin
items.cause: Not chilled long enough, or the caramel has stuck and solidified
items.fix: Refrigerate for at least 6 hours. A 10-second dip in warm water can help release a stubborn custard by warming the caramel enough to liquefy.

items.cause: Milk was too hot when it hit the eggs, or baked at too high a temperature
items.fix: Make sure the milk is steaming but not boiling before mixing with the eggs. Keep the oven at 150°C fan and use the water bath throughout.

content.text: Crème caramel belongs to a large family of caramel-lined custard moulds that spans French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Latin American cooking. The Spanish flan, the Portuguese pudim flan, and the French crème caramel are all regional versions of the same technique. In France the dish became associated with restaurant cooking in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries; in Spain and Latin America it remained a domestic preparation. It reached British home cooking via both French restaurant influence and the mid-twentieth century interest in continental desserts.
